data.title: 2 Absurdly Cheap REITs With An Average 6.3% Yield To Grow Your Retirement Income
data.description:
Introduction

The on and off again tariffs have wreaked havoc on the overall market the past few weeks. And the trade war between the U.S. and China is heating up with China now imposing 125% tariffs on U.S. goods, up from 84% previously.

As a result, the back and forth between the two countries will likely result in increased market volatility in the coming weeks. However, long-term investors should ignore the noise and continue to invest wisely.

For retirees, now is a great time to position your portfolio to take advantage of the dip in equities and grow your income stream in the process. Most retirees rely more on income and care less about growth as a result of age.

In this article, I list two insanely cheap REITs with sound fundamentals that investors, particularly retirees, should consider for their portfolios.

Why REITs Make Great Income Vehicles

Before getting into the two companies in this article, I wanted to talk about why REITs make good investments for retirees, particularly now with increased volatility.

As someone who considers themselves great at seeing and finding opportunities, now is an opportune time to build your income stream as a retiree. And REITs are where you should look.

For starters, if you're a retiree, you most likely are in search of higher yielding investments instead of growth because of your age. When you become older, life priorities shift, likely the same for your investments.

In the chart below, you can see REITs currently have much higher yields than stocks & bonds in the U.S., and globally. At this moment bonds are also attractive in the U.S. at 2.3%. But buying quality REITs that own iconic assets could be considered more attractive.

With President Trump pausing tariffs for 90 days with the exception of China, many are likely wondering what may happen next. Until then, I expect the market to remain volatile.

Tariffs are also expected to be highly inflationary, which may prompt the FED to keep interest rates elevated for much longer.

I do suspect we will see at least one cut this year, but this remains to be seen, especially if tariffs are reinstated in the coming months.

The good thing is real estate has been known to outperform during periods of high inflation as well.

And lastly, REITs have delivered strong returns and beat the overall stock & bond markets since 1991. As most know, REITs do have periods of underperformance, specifically more recently in 2022 when interest rates were hiked the fastest in history.

But other sectors can have periods of underperformance as well, depending on what's going on in the overall market or economy.

But the key is to find quality REITs with strong fundamentals that will likely continue to reward you with income.

This may slow during times of uncertainty, but these two REITs I expect will continue to cover their dividend, at least for the near to medium term.

Without further ado, let's get into the two REITs retirees should consider.

#1 American Assets Trust (AAT) - 7.35% Yield

The first REIT is one I think doesn't get enough love or coverage, American Assets Trust. AAT is based in San Diego, California and owns office and mixed-use assets.

Since the pandemic, the office sector hasn't been seen as very attractive, many still recovering from the unexpected crisis.

I last covered AAT back in February. And since then, the REIT has sold off, down over 20%. This is in comparison to the S&P (SP500), who's down roughly 15% over the same period.

With a yield well over 7% backed by a solid payout ratio of roughly 69%, now may be a great time to pick up some shares.

They were also able to grow their funds available for distribution from $133.4 million to $140.4 million year-over-year, solid considering the challenging economic backdrop.

Although office REITs are considered riskier with companies still allowing employees to work from home, impacting their occupancies, AAT's property ownership in affluent markets like San Diego & Hawaii is what makes them particularly attractive.

During 2024, AAT delivered a solid performance despite economic headwinds for REITs with higher for longer interest rates. In Q4, they beat on both top & bottom lines. For the year, the REIT brought in a record $2.58 in FFO, their third consecutive year.

And despite 2025 expected to be a challenging year with an expected decline in FFO to $1.87 - $2.01, this is still more than enough to cover their annualized dividend of $1.36, which they raised 1.5% in early February.

2026 is expected to be a better year with the stabilization of one of their San Diego properties and future developments projected to provide $0.30 bottom-line growth once 93% occupancy is reached.

American Assets Trust is also targeting growth in their multifamily portfolio, providing diversification away from the office sector.

In late February, AAT acquired a 192-unit apartment community in San Diego for $67.9 million that was 93% leased using available cash on hand. Additionally, their balance sheet was solid, with no remaining debt this year and none maturing until 2027.

This allows AAT to continue making investments and gives them some flexibility should interest rates remain higher for longer.

Additionally, their forward P/FFO ratio of just 9.70x using their 2025 midpoint of $1.94, this also makes them attractive.

Despite 2025 likely continuing to be a volatile year, limiting their upside, 2026 is likely to be much better, with management expecting $0.30 accretion to their bottom line.

Until then, investors get paid a 7.35% yield well-covered by cash flows while they wait for potential upside.

#2 Rexford Industrial (REXR) - 5.18% Yield

Keeping it in Southern California is Rexford Industrial Realty. As a result of tariffs, REXR and peers have sold off drastically since my last thesis, tripling the S&P's drop of 10% at the time of writing.

This is a result of them owning and developing warehouses, which store and ship goods and are usually sourced from countries outside the U.S.

But their sell-off should be viewed as an opportunity. To be clear, I think office and industrial REITs are riskier than say retail REITs like Agree Realty (ADC) and Realty Income (O).

But their fundamentals remain strong and provide significant upside over the long term. And REXR is no exception. Despite headwinds, the REIT was able to grow rental revenue from $207.9 million to $239.7 million year-over-year.

What also was impressive is they managed to grow both core FFO & AFFO despite a sizable decline in same-store portfolio occupancy. This declined from 97.1% to 94.1% over the same period.

So, if investors are worried about the REIT's dividend safety, this was well-covered with a 73% payout ratio.

The company also raised their dividend by a solid 3% in February to $0.43 a share, remaining committed to dividend growth. And although dividend growth may slow until headwinds abate, their yield and price drop make them particularly attractive.

For 2024, acquisitions totaled $1.5 billion. And despite no pipeline, Rexford does expect to see 1% NOI growth from redevelopment and repositioning projects in 2025.

Guidance has been established at $2.37 - $2.41, a growth rate of 2.1% from 2024's $2.34. Moreover, management established a $300 million share buyback program, which I expect them to take advantage of REXR's price drop.

At the current price, the REIT has a forward P/FFO multiple of just 13.73x. This is well-below the industrial REIT average of 20x FFO. Currently, their upside over 40% and well-covered dividend yield makes them worth considering at current levels.

Their balance sheet also supports their dividend with a leverage ratio of 4.6x. Their debt was also well-staggered and robust liquidity available.

This included nearly $1 billion available on their credit facility and $400 million in cash and unsettled forward equity expecting to be settled by the upcoming quarter.

Risks

As a result of tariffs, recession fears have picked up in recent weeks. If one does come to a fruition, both REITs could see further downside as this would likely cause occupancies to drop and a decline in M&A activity.

Moreover, both portfolios are concentrated in Southern California, making them riskier investments as SOCAL is known for natural disasters like earthquakes and wildfires.

This also subjects them to higher costs and regulatory challenges, which may be viewed as risky for potential investors. This is something investors should keep an eye on going forward.

Conclusion

Despite both AAT & REXR being in riskier sectors that expose them to further downside from future tariffs, their strong fundamentals, yields above 5% and well-covered dividends make them attractive for retirees looking to grow their income.

Moreover, you get paid well while you wait for headwinds to abate and could potentially see significant upside, likely over the medium to long term.

While 2025 will likely provide further volatility, investors should consider taking advantage of the dips in both American Assets Trust and Rexford Industrial Realty's share prices.

data.title: Bicara: Innovative Precision Tumor Targeting
data.description:
Many therapies in oncology show initial promise, but fail to meet the twin hurdles of tumor resistance and toxicity. It remains the central challenge to find approaches that thread this needle - with potent efficacy and a manageable safety profile. Against this demanding backdrop, Bicara Therapeutics (BCAX) presents a compelling early-stage investment consideration. I believe that Bicara's focused strategy around its lead bifunctional antibody candidate ficerafusp alfa is a scientifically differentiated approach with significant potential for use in hard-to-treat cancers such as Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma.

Bicara isn't boiling the ocean. It's focused on one rational biological mechanism: Targeting the epidermal growth factor receptor and neutralizing transforming growth factor-beta directly within the tumor microenvironment. In this dual-action design, embodied in ficerafusp alfa, EGFR inhibition targets the tumor cell directly while dismantling the immunosuppressive shield of TGF-beta. This localized two-pronged assault may overcome resistance mechanisms to single-target therapies and provide an environment favorable to immune-mediated tumor destruction.

Financial Update

Bicara's financial position provides an overview of its operational status and runway following its transition to a public company. The company had $489.7 million in cash and cash equivalents as at December 31, 2024. This is a big jump from $230.4 million at the end of 2023, helped by its IPO proceeds. Importantly, on the basis of its current operational and development plans, Bicara projects this cash reserve will cover its activities through the first half of 2029 and give it a large runway to advance its clinical programs.

Reflecting the ramp-up of clinical activities, including the pivotal FORTIFI-HN01 trial initiation, research and development expenses increased. For the fourth quarter in 2024, R&D expenses were $19.9 million, compared to $10.6 million in 2023. For the full year, R&D spending was $63.6 million in 2024 versus $30.6 million in 2023. The increase was largely driven by costs associated with initiating the large Phase 2 / 3 trial and advancing work on the ongoing Phase 1 / 1b study of ficerafusp alfa.

For the fourth quarter and the full year ended on December 31, 2024, G&A expenses were $6.8 million and $18.8 million respectively. This is compared to $3.1 million versus $9.3 million for the same periods in 2023. It grew mainly because of increased personnel costs and professional fees related to public company readiness and operations. Thus, the company's net loss widened to $21 million for the fourth quarter and $68 million for the full year in 2024, from $12.4 million and $52.0 million for the same periods in 2023 (noting the 2023 full-year figure included a non-cash expense related to preferred stock valuation).

Dual-Threat Strategy

Ficerafusp alfa is the core asset and the first-in-class bifunctional antibody. Such a molecule combines an anti-EGFR antibody component with a TGF-B trap (extracellular domain of the human TGF-B receptor II). A well-validated target in many cancers including HNSCC, EGFR promotes tumor cell proliferation and survival. Therapies focusing exclusively on EGFR, for example, cetuximab, are often resistant and lack durability. TGF-b plays an important immunosuppressive role in the tumor microenvironment and inhibits the attack of cancer by T-cells and natural killer cells.

By physically linking the EGFR blockade and TGF-B neutralization, ficerafusp alfa targets its dual activity at the EGFR-expressed tumor site. In this targeted delivery, efficacy is maximized while systemic toxicities of less targeted TGF-B inhibition may be minimized. Preclinical work showed that this strategy could outperform anti-EGFR antibodies alone in preventing tumor recurrence and restoring immune activation. The human clinical studies test this hypothesis. Mainly focused on an ongoing Phase 2/3 trial of ficerafusp alfa in combination with pembrolizumab as the first-line therapy in recurrent/metastatic HNSCC in the HPV-negative population. The dose expansion cohorts in the Phase 1/1b study continue to explore its utility in other settings, including squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal and possibly other squamous cell tumors.

Series C Upswing

The $165 million Series C financing announced in December 2023 was a significant milestone. Beyond the large capital infusion, timing was critical. That follows the presentation of positive interim data from the Phase 1/1b study of ficerafusp alfa at major medical conferences such as the American Society of Clinical Oncology and the European Society for Medical Oncology earlier in the year. These presentations demonstrated clinically meaningful anti-tumor activity and a tolerable safety profile in the challenging first-line, HPV-negative R/M HNSCC population combined with pembrolizumab. All this new capital from top-tier investors was earmarked to accelerate the development of ficerafusp alfa and initiate pivotal trials.

Key Conference Data

Bicara has presented encouraging, early clinical data at major oncology meetings. In interim data from the dose expansion cohort of ficerafusp alfa plus pembrolizumab in first-line R/M HNSCC, the company presented interim data at ASCO 2023. The results revealed promising anti-tumor activity mainly in HPV-negative patients with poor prognosis. A June 2024 update with longer follow-up maintained this trend with a 64% overall and 18% complete response rate in patients with HPV-negative tumors compared to historical pembrolizumab monotherapy rates. Median progression-free survival was 9.8 months, and the median duration of response and overall survival were not reached, suggesting durable responses. Similarly, data presented at ESMO 2022 showed the combination was well tolerated and active. Data presented at the ASCO GI Symposium in January 2025 showed a 25% confirmed overall response rate in heavily pre-treated advanced squamous cell carcinoma of the anal canal patients with pembrolizumab. These consistent updates with encouraging efficacy and manageable safety instill confidence in the therapeutic concept.

Pivotal Trial Launch

A major step forward was recently announced: the first patients enrolled in FORTIFI-HN01. It is the pivotal Phase 2/3 trial of ficerafusp alfa plus pembrolizumab versus pembrolizumab plus placebo in first-line R/M HNSCC patients with HPV-negative tumors expressing PD-L1 (CPS ≥1). The initiation of this registration-enabling trial marks a turning point in the development of ficerafusp alfa from exploratory to potential market approval. Success in this trial may establish a new chemotherapy-free standard of care for this high unmet need population.

Upcoming Risks and Competitors

Investment in clinical-stage biotechnology involves inherent risks. The primary risk for Bicara is clinical trial failure. Promising Phase 1 / 1b data aside, larger, randomized Phase 2 / 3 trials are needed to establish efficacy and safety, and many drug candidates fail here. It also involves regulatory risk. Approval by bodies like the FDA requires robust data indicating a good benefit-risk profile. Biologic manufacturing scale-up can be challenging.

Interestingly, although ficerafusp alfa has a relatively unique dual-targeting mechanism, other companies are exploring EGFR inhibitors, TGF-b inhibitors, or different combination strategies in HNSCC and other solid tumors. Those existing treatments - chemotherapy and checkpoint inhibitors such as pembrolizumab and nivolumab - constitute the current standard of care against which Bicara must demonstrate superiority. Other bispecific antibodies to different combinations are also under development by competitors, although Bicara's EGFR/TGF-b approach seems to be unique. Its success is dependent on ficerafusp alfa demonstrating clear advantages over these alternatives in efficacy, durability, or safety.

Gauging Market Potential

Market research reports estimate that the overall HNSCC market size was between $1.0 and $1.8 billion in 2023/2024 and could grow to $2.4 to $2.8 billion by 2029-2034. Specifically, the recurrent/metastatic market was worth around $1.6 billion in 2024 and was expected to reach $3.8 billion by 2035. HPV-negative HNSCC constitutes a significant proportion of these cases, especially in those with poorer prognoses that are routinely treated in the first-line setting for recurrent or metastatic diseases. The incidence rates suggest tens of thousands of new addressable patients per year in both the US and Europe.

Treatment costs for advanced HNSCC are high. Immunotherapies like pembrolizumab and targeted therapies like cetuximab may cost upwards of six figures per patient annually. Studies have quoted annual costs broadly, with some analyses citing costs exceeding $70,000-$100,000 a decade ago and modern combination therapies often much higher. The market for the ficerafusp alfa combination is potentially sizable if a future price point is competitive with existing branded oncology biologics (conservatively, $180,000 per patient per year).

This translates to 7,500 patients if we assume an average of 30,000 addressable first-line R/M HPV-negative HNSCC patients annually in the US and major EU markets, and Bicara achieves a plausible peak market share of 25% in this segment. That equates to a potential peak annual revenue of $1.3 billion at this first indication of $180,000/year. A fraction of the larger HNSCC market or expansion into other EGFR/TGF-b-driven tumors (such as certain lung or colorectal cancers studied preclinically) would significantly increase this potential. It is an extremely speculative calculation and dependent on clinical success, regulatory approval, pricing, and market adoption, but it illustrates the enormous commercial potential of ficerafusp alfa if and when it succeeds.

Concluding Thoughts

Bicara Therapeutics is a targeted, scientifically rational investment case within the high-risk/high-reward biotech sector. As its lead asset, ficerafusp alfa uses a differentiated dual-targeting mechanism to overcome key resistance pathways in solid tumors. With substantial venture rounds and a successful IPO, the company is well-funded to pursue its pivotal trial in first-line R/M HPV-negative HNSCC. Good early clinical data implies meaningful activity with manageable safety in combination with pembrolizumab. Clinical and regulatory hurdles remain significant, but the market opportunity for HNSCC alone is huge. Investors who want exposure to newer oncology approaches with high upside potential should watch Bicara Therapeutics closely as its pivotal data develops.

data.title: Oil Bulls Should Pay Close Attention To Forum Energy Technologies
data.description:
Forum Energy Technologies, Inc. (FET) is a global manufacturer of products for the oil and gas, industrial and renewable energy industries. It operates two segments - Drilling and Completions and Artificial Lift and Downhole. The former segment sells capital equipment and consumable products used in the drilling process, subsea remotely operated vehicles, hydraulic fracturing pumps, cooling systems, high-pressure flexible hoses and flow iron, wireline cable and coded tubing. The latter segment manufactures and supplies products and solutions for the artificial lift, well construction, production and infrastructure markets. Basically anything to do with the operation of oil wells that the general public has no clue about, FET is involved in it. This is not a business that would be considered a hot and growing market by most investors. But what this company lacks in an engaging story it has in spades with its ability to generate cash flow. This makes it a good value play candidate for investors who are bullish on oil and like the speculative upside potential and volatility of a microcap stock. As one can see, FET has historically been quite volatile, to put it nicely:

Reviewing the company's full-year results, 2024 revenue was $816.4 million, a 10.5% increase over 2023's revenue of $738.9 million. The gross margin grew 24.8% from $204.2 million to $255 million year-over-year. Adjusted EBITDA grew 49% from $67.1 million to $100 million. FET booked a net loss of $135.3 million for 2024 compared to a net loss of $18.9 million in 2023. But that variance is explained by a $119.1 million impairment in the coiled tubing product line. While FET doesn't look that great from an EPS perspective, its true value comes from its ability to generate cash flow. Operating cash flow grew from $8.2 million in 2023 to $92.2 million in 2024. The company generated free cash flow of $105.1 million in 2024 compared to $1.6 million in 2023. So FET is trading at less than 2x its trailing FCF.

This outstanding ability to generate cash flow has enabled FET's Board of Directors to approve an aggressive $75 million share repurchase program. $2 million worth of shares were repurchased in January 2025. Couple that with net insider buying and FET has a tight float with the company and insiders having the ability and incentive to make that float even tighter.

If there is a black cloud hanging over FET, it's the stagnant growth that was masked by the acquisition of Variperm, a specialist in thermally compliant downhole completions systems & tools, in January 2024. This stagnation becomes more apparent when looking at results by segment:

Drilling and Completions revenue was down 6.5% from 2023 to 2024 while Artificial Lift and Downhole revenue increased 46% on the strength of the acquisition. The Artificial Lift and Downhole segment has far superior margins, generating $48.9 million in operating income in 2024 compared to $17.8 million for the Drilling and Completions segment despite having less revenue. If the company can continue this trend towards generating revenue in the higher margin Artificial Lift and Downhole segment, gross margins will continue to improve, which will have a positive impact on the bottom line.

However, this lack of growth becomes more apparent when reviewing 2025 guidance as the year-over-year comparison is apples to apples. Full year 2025 adjusted EBITDA is expected to be in the $85 to $105 million range while free cash flow is expected to be in the $40 to $60 million range. The midpoint of guidance results in a 5% decline in EBITDA from 2024 while free cash flow is expected to drop by more than half. Order backlog dropped from $241.6 million at the end of 2023 to $213.5 million at the end of 2024. However, bookings, which consist of written orders or commitments for FET's products or related services increased to $780.3 million at the end of 2024 versus $724.3 million at the end of 2023. So there is mixed data related to how FET will fair in 2025, but don't expect a spectacular year relative to 2024.

Conclusion: Value investors who are bullish on oil should consider an investment in FET

FET is a cheap stock with a lot of potential to do well, but is far from perfect. Its suite of products makes it imperative to the energy industry. While the company remains optimistic about the industry in the long term, it's clear from its guidance that it believes 2025 will be a mediocre year. This is why I say FET is an appropriate investment mainly for oil bulls. If an investor is going to invest in energy companies anyways, they may consider allocating some of that cash to FET as opposed to a producer. If the oil business is robust in 2026 and beyond, FET will see strong growth. I believe that FET is well positioned to outperform producers in an energy bull market. However, for someone who is not bullish on oil, they can see FET's strong cash generating ability but will also see its flat organic growth and be less impressed.

While the Board may have approved a $75 million stock repurchase plan, in reality FET won't buy anywhere near that amount of shares in 2025. FCF is expected to be only around $50 million and the CEO stated that half of the FCF generated in 2025 will be used to repay debt. As interest expense of $31.5 million in 2024 was a big driver of the variance between EBITDA and net income, this is a wise move. Investors shouldn't expect such aggressive buying of stock in 2025, but should expect the company to be in a better position to report positive EPS in 2026 upon a reduction in interest. A combination of a low FCF along with positive net income should finally get the stock moving higher. Assuming the oil business is in a neutral to bullish environment. I am placing a speculative buy on FET but will remain on the sidelines for now until I have a more bullish outlook on the energy sector.

data.title: Accenture: Buy The Trump Slump (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro

Accenture plc (ACN) has been a target of the DOGE and as a result, the stock has plunged recently. I believe this is a massive overreaction that creates a buying opportunity. As I will discuss below, key technical bottoming signs have appeared and so a bullish reversal is likely moving forward. As for the fundamentals, Q2 earnings were respectable. Guidance for their Q3 and full year 2025 showed off the company's confidence in their outlook as strong resilience is expected despite Trump headwinds. While revenue growth is expected to remain relatively stable in USD terms for their Q3 and the company has raised their guidance on select metrics for the full year, the P/S ratio has plummeted since its high earlier this year. This suggests undervaluation of the stock. As a result, I initiate Accenture at a buy rating on the basis of technical bottoming and fundamental resilience.

Technical Analysis

The Basics

Trends, Support & Resistance

Accenture currently remains in a downtrend that began back in late February. This trend line seems to have pressured the stock significantly this month and may continue to do so moving forward. The sole support level in this timeframe is at around 280. This was the level of a bullish engulfing pattern that occurred lately. As you can see, there have been numerous candle wicks in this area. Three resistance levels are currently above the stock, of which the nearest is at around 300. This was a downside gap earlier this month and seems to have already acted as resistance once. The next source of resistance is in the mid-310s. This level was supported in early to mid-March while contributing to resistance very early this month. Lastly, the mid-320s is likely to be significant resistance with that level being the major late March gap down that occurred on overwhelming volume.

Moving Averages & Indicators

The 50-day SMA had a death cross with the 200-day SMA just very recently, showing long-term pessimism in the stock. The deviation between the two lines continues to grow and so downward momentum is still building. The stock also broke beneath the 200-day SMA's support in early March, which should have been a signal of things to come. The latest crossover for the EMAs was back in late February, and it was a bearish one. The 9 day EMA has been consistently below the 21 day EMA ever since, demonstrating sustained bearishness. The gap between the EMAs has at least stayed relatively steady and so near term downward momentum hasn't been accelerating.

Accenture stock broke beneath the Bollinger Band's midline in late February as a signal of weakness. Recall, that the downtrend started back then as well. The midline seems to have contributed as resistance very early this month as a confirmation signal of the downtrend. For the MACD, there have been numerous non-meaningful crossovers lately showing indecision in the stock. The histogram has also been very flat. The major signal for the MACD is that the indicator has been recovering since its late March low while the stock has continued to drop. This is a sign that strength is building underneath the surface and so there is positive divergence. For the RSI, the indicator now has a reading of 35.05 after being below 30 earlier this month. The bears have therefore lost some ground to the bulls. The RSI also now seems to be in a bit of an uptrend that suggests further strength. Therefore, there is positive divergence here as well with two consecutive higher lows in the indicator. Lastly, the stochastics just had a bearish crossover as the %K's trajectory has turned negative again. While this shows a stalling recovery in the stock, both lines still remain above 20 as a sign of weakened bears. The stochastics also shows bullish divergence with its low this month holding significantly above its March low.

Takeaway

The chart shows Accenture being in a strong downtrend and the SMAs just flashed a death cross. These are worrying signs, but I believe there are reasons to be hopeful if you're a bull on the stock. The bullish engulfing pattern identified above and the positive divergence in the MACD, RSI, and stochastics suggest that a bullish reversal is likely to occur. The bottom may be in or near as the indicators have seen broad strengthening for some time. While it may be more risky to buy in before the downtrend is broken, I believe brave investors may be rewarded for jumping in with only these bottoming signals to work with.

Fibonacci

Fan Lines

Using the early February peak and the early April low as the anchor, resistance fan lines are given in the chart above. As you can see, during the recent bounce in the stock, the 23.6% fan line may have contributed significantly to resistance. Multiple candlesticks were rejected by this fan line in a short period of time. If a bullish reversal is to continue, the stock must break this fan line quite quickly as the stock may be pressured below the bullish engulfing pattern in the near future. The 38.2% fan line is also now moving near 300 and so there are still obstacles for Accenture even if a bullish reversal does occur.

Projection

Using the early February high again, the late March low, and the retracement to around 320, the Fibonacci Projection has provided potential support zones as shown above. The nearest level at 61.8% is very near the 250 price level in the stock. Therefore, if the bullish reversal fails and the declines continue, this area may represent a strong support level or floor for the stock moving forward. The next projection level at 100% is quite far below and so a break below 250 would be a concerning sign for the bulls if it does, in fact, happen.

Ichimoku Cloud (Advanced)

Accenture stock is far below the cloud currently, and so general bearishness is quite strong at this point. The Leading Span A also crossed below the Leading Span B in early April and so the cloud itself is also bearish now. Furthermore, as the Leading Span A continues to fall into late May, resistance may be worrying about the stock as the cloud is also thickening substantially. Therefore, it could get increasingly difficult for Accenture to break above the cloud moving forward. The Conversion Line crossed below the baseline in late February to show that near term momentum had shifted bearish. The deviation between these two lines has decreased a bit since late March, however, and so downward momentum seems to have slowed modestly. Lastly, the Lagging Span is currently significantly below the stock price of early March, but its gap with the historic stock price has decreased since mid-February. Therefore, the Lagging Span confirms the downtrend, but it is increasingly evident that bearish momentum is on the decline.

Fundamental Analysis

Earnings

Quarterly Results

Accenture reported their FY2025 Q2 earnings back on March 20th and showed, in my view, respectable results. The company posted revenues of $16.7 billion, up 5% YoY and 8.5% in local currency terms. This figure exceeded consensus expectations by $49.70 million, showing strength in the business. As you can see in the chart above, growth did slow QoQ as the growth recovery shows some signs of stalling. Another key figure is their Generative AI bookings. For their Q2, they recorded $1.4 billion in new bookings, up significantly from the prior year period's $600 million figure. This shows AI is driving substantial growth for the company. For EPS, they reported an adjusted figure of $2.82, up 2% YoY. This figure beat estimates slightly by $0.01. Operating cash flow saw healthy increases as they generated $2.85 billion versus the year ago quarter's $2.10 billion. As shown above, the 35.81% growth rate is still a deceleration from the previous quarter, however. Nonetheless, I believe the business remains overall healthy with both revenue and operating cash flow growth remaining resilient.

Guidance

For their FY2025 Q3, they are expecting to see revenues ranging from $16.9 billion to $17.5 billion. In Accenture's local currency, revenue growth is expected to be between 3% and 7%. The midpoint at 5% represents a slowdown from Q2's 8.5% growth rate. In USD terms, the midpoint of $17.2 billion is an 4.2% increase over the prior year period's $16.5 billion in revenue. This is a minor deceleration versus Q2's 5% growth rate in USD terms. Operational growth is therefore expected to moderate slightly for the company. While I won't go into depth for their full-year guidance, there are some notable developments. In their Q2 report, they raised their local currency revenue growth guidance to between 5% and 7% from 4% to 7% and also raised their EPS guidance to between $12.55 and $12.79 from $12.43 to $12.79. This indicates strengthened confidence over the longer-term outlook for the business. Note that this guidance comes as the U.S. government is threatening spending cuts on consulting. Therefore, Accenture is demonstrating high resilience as these guidance figures indicate that they are in a strong position to weather these headwinds.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio [P/S]

The P/S ratio has collapsed since its 2025 high earlier in the year. The multiple was at levels of over 3.6 and now only sits at 2.67. I believe this is a massive overreaction that makes the stock undervalued at current levels. As discussed above, revenue growth in their Q2 has dropped QoQ and the growth recovery seems to be stalling. However, the figure remains near average levels relative to the past few years. Furthermore, Q3 guidance only shows a very minor deceleration for revenue growth in USD terms. In addition, Accenture actually raised their full-year local currency revenue growth guidance despite the U.S. Government's threats of spending cuts. Therefore, the outlook shows strong resilience in their business moving forward. Despite this, the P/S ratio has plummeted to its lowest level since early calendar 2023, showing that the current pessimism in the stock is overdone. Currently, the Information Technology sector has a median P/S of 2.52 and so Accenture is at a mild premium to peers. Given their strong position in the consulting industry, I believe the company can easily justify an even greater premium.

Conclusion

In the above analysis, key technical bottoming signals were highlighted. The bullish engulfing pattern along with the positive divergence signals in the MACD, RSI, and stochastics show that the bottom is likely in for the stock. As for the fundamentals, most recent earnings were respectable. Furthermore, guidance showed resilience as revenue growth is expected to remain relatively stable for Q3 in USD terms. Guidance for local currency revenue growth and EPS was also raised for the full year, demonstrating a confident management team despite Trump headwinds. While this is the case, the P/S ratio has shown largely irrational contraction as investors seem to have overreacted to the news. With the selloff coming to an end according to the technicals and the fundamental setup now being attractive, I initiate Accenture at a buy rating.

data.title: IHS Holding: Solid Competitive Advantage And Growth Catalysts
data.description:
Investment action

I give a buy rating recommendation for IHS Holding (NYSE:IHS). The business has solid competitive advantages, visible growth catalysts, and a cleaner balance sheet today. I don’t think the market has fully priced in the upside yet, and if IHS generates the adj. EBITDA that consensus expects, I see very attractive upsides from here.

Company description

IHS is the 5th largest independent owner and operator of telecommunications infrastructure. As of 4Q24, IHS owned 39,229 towers across Africa and Latin America, where it enables mobile network operators to provide coverage to end consumers through long-term lease agreements. As such, IHS's business model has visible recurring revenue streams, backed by multi-year contracts with major carriers like MTN, Airtel, and Orange. Nigeria is IHS’s largest market, accounting for 58% of FY24 revenue and 63% of adj EBITDA.

Competitive Advantages: Scale, Contractual Visibility, and De-Risked Economics

What stands out most about IHS is its scale advantage. As shown above (4Q24 earnings presentation), IHS is the 5th largest independent owner and operator in the world, and it is the largest operator in Africa and the dominant player in six of the eight countries it is in. In Nigeria alone, IHS operates more than 16,000 towers. This scale and underlying reach (i.e., regional coverage) are a major value proposition to tenants (i.e., the telecos) because they want to be able to reach as many consumers as possible. At the same time, I believe it significantly reduces the threat of competition. First of all, the barriers to entry are already steep. A new player needs many regulatory approvals and a lot of capital to get the infrastructure up and get tenants to sign with them. The first two are the easy part; the latter is where the IHS scale deters it. Once a tenant signs an agreement with IHS to have coverage over a region, it is unlikely that they will sign with another player for the same coverage (why pay twice the cost?). Hence, with IHS having the number 1 position and already having agreements with major telecos, I think it will be very hard to undermine IHS's market position.

Scale also translates directly into margin. Because incremental tenants can be added to existing infrastructure with little additional cost, each additional volume (tenant) tends to carry very high incremental margins. You can clearly see the impact of scale on margins from IHS financials. Nigeria, IHS's largest market (note: I use revenue as a determination of scale for regional comparison), has the highest EBITDA margin vs. smaller regions like Sub-Saharan Africa (which has a higher margin than the Middle East and North Africa). This ability to scale without materially expanding cost is a huge positive. As IHS scales larger, it has more cash/financial muscle to reinvest to build more towers to further strengthen its market position/scale advantage, which forms a virtuous cycle.

Another important point that has further improved IHS's competitive advantage is the shift in contract structures around power. IHS has moved the majority of its business to either power pass-through (e.g., South Africa) or power indexation (e.g., Nigeria), which significantly reduces its exposure to diesel price volatility. From the 4Q24 earnings call:

Through our contract renewals, we have moved the significant majority of our business to either power pass through like in South Africa, or power indexation like in Nigeria, closer aligning our operating model to the steel and grass model in more developed countries.

Growth Tailwinds

IHS's growth outlook is also well supported by strong tailwinds. The clearest near-term catalyst is Nigeria, where macro conditions are starting to improve. Most notably, in January 2025, Nigeria’s telecom regulator approved a 50% tariff increase for mobile carriers (note this is the first pricing adjustment in more than 12 years). This is a very significant development because it gives carriers like MTN and Airtel more cash to reinvest in their networks. This means more CAPEX, which means more demand for towers, and that is music to IHS's ears. In fact, this translation to demand is already happening—IHS had signed an extended contract with Airtel Nigeria for 3,950 new tenancies (per 4Q24 earnings transcript).

Another tailwind is the secular demand tailwind. The African telecom tower market is expected to grow from an installed base of 208,310 units in 2024 to 261,210 units by 2029, which is about ~4.63% CAGR. I believe this is a fair expectation because there is increasing mobile data consumption, which is expected to grow by ~40% annually until 2025 (~2x global average). Additionally, in Nigeria, the government, in collaboration with the private sector, plans to deploy 7,000 new telecom towers in 2025 to enhance network coverage and digital connectivity. These developments clearly indicate significant demand for telecom infrastructure in the region. Given IHS's scale and expertise, I believe it is very well-positioned to capitalize on these.

Balance sheet

It is also very encouraging that IHS now has a much stronger cash flow and balance sheet profile. In 4Q24, IHS generated ~$107 million in ALFCF (adjusted levered free cash flow), which was up ~23% sequentially from 3Q24, and for the full year, ALFCF came in at $304 million. This gave IHS more flexibility to deleverage its balance sheet—net leverage ended 4Q24 at 3.7x, down from 3.9x in 3Q24. Management also guided that it expects to end FY25 in the lower half of its 3x to 4x target range, even without relying on additional disposals. This is important because it means that EBITDA growth and internally generated cash are now enough to continue deleveraging without needing to sell assets just to stay on track, and IHS can now be more aggressive in investing in growth CAPEX.

And with ~$900 million in liquidity, if the business continues to generate ALFCF as it does now, plus the growth potential, IHS may soon have the headroom to consider buybacks or even introduce a dividend policy, both of which were floated on the call. From the 4Q24 earnings call:

As previously indicated, excess cash following implementation of our strategic initiatives is expected to be primarily used to reduce debt while also considering share buybacks and/or introducing a dividend policy.

Valuation

Using consensus adj EBITDA estimates, which have been historically accurate over the past 4 years, I see a 61% upside to IHS's share price. Using consensus estimates, IHS is expected to generate $1.17 billion in adj EBITDA, which is ~6% CAGR vs. FY24. This is not a high hurdle considering the growth catalysts I mentioned above. I used a 5x NTM EBITDA to value IHS in FY28, which I also think is not an aggressive assumption since this is IHS's historical average. The main reason why the share price upside is huge is because IHS is very leveraged. The business is in a net debt position of ~$3.3 billion as of 4Q24, which is more than 60% of its current enterprise value.

Risk

The biggest near-term risk, in my view, remains Nigeria. The naira remains volatile, and any further devaluation could pressure reported revenue and cash repatriation. In addition, although the Nigerian macro is getting better, it needs to be stable and growing. Any negative macro development will limit or impede CapEx investments, which is bad for IHS. Additionally, regulatory changes such as forced tower sharing mandates, price caps on leasing rates, or restrictions on foreign cash repatriation could negatively impact IHS’s margins or growth. Lastly, although power indexation and pass-through mechanisms are in place, unexpected spikes in diesel or grid instability could still impact margin conversion in certain markets.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for IHS. My view is that the business has a very strong competitive advantage and a good growth outlook, which the market hasn’t fully priced in yet. The healthier balance sheet also means IHS is in a better position to reinvest for growth. If the company executes and the valuation stays at this level, the share price could rerate meaningfully higher.

data.title: Carrefour: Good Short-Term Returns, I Remain At 'BUY'
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

It seems to me that few heed my ratings on some French and European companies - Carrefour SA (OTCPK:CRRFY) (OTCPK:CRERF) is one of them. In this article, I will attempt to once again show you Carrefour as an attractive investment, this time using the 1Q25 results to show you why the company is attractively positioned for growth.

Carrefour recently gave us its 1Q25 results, and the company appreciated as a result of this. Remember, much of my position is bought at the current, or even a lower cost base than we see today. I have a very significant 2% portfolio stake in Carrefour, so when it comes to this one, I have put my money where my mouth is and expect a very significant medium and long-term upside.

I have a high degree of success investing in grocers and FMCG companies across the world. I'm talking about companies like Kroger (KR), Axfood (OTCPK:AXFOY), Ahold Delhaize (OTCQX:ADRNY), and others. These companies form the backbone of something that people need daily, and it becomes only clearer when I am travelling across Europe, Africa, or South America and find myself in one of the company's directly owned or third-party owned outlets and stores.

Carrefour is an excellent company. The 12% earnings decline in 2024 which was partially responsible for what you see in valuation, should, in my view, not take your eyes away from "the prize" here, the prize being the fact that the company currently trade at 8.21x P/E normalized, with a current 2024/2025 dividend yield of over 8.5% and that is sustainable based on earnings.

I will show you what the company has done in 2024 and use this to justify my valuation of the company long-term. Since my last article, the company is up 6.32% compared to an S&P that's down 13.05%, you can find that article and comparison here.

Carrefour - 1Q25 enhances and confirms the upside we're seeing.

Multinational retail and grocery is something that I am "into". This one is French and has a history of well over 60 years behind it. From A to Z, Carrefour operates everything from the well-known hypermarket format, to supermarkets, convenience stores, and even small cash stores, and in some places, cash-and-carry-outlets in addition to the obvious e-commerce that most retailers seem to do these days.

Operations are centered in France, with secondary markets including Western and Central Europe, Eastern Europe, the Middle East, and Africa. Where I currently am in eastern Africa, the company is considered to be one of the premier upper-middle-class outlets for shopping for groceries, and it's where I do most of my daily getting of groceries. Brazil is also very important for the company, given its Atacadao brand.

2024 results highlight the company's challenging market conditions and the impact of inflation and consumption pressure. This has resulted in increased competition among retailers, which has only honed the aggressive positioning that the companies need to apply, as well as the optimized cost structures currently in play.

However, Carrefour managed significant advantages and positives during the year. In France, the company invested heavily to improve pricing, which saw operating income up over 5% to over €1B for that market alone. That's a major milestone achievement, with its ROI margin up 5 bps. The company did this while successfully integrating Cora & Match, and has already confirmed synergies of over €125M until 2027E, which will improve this even further.

Spain, the company's second-most important market, led positive market share trends at the end of the year, with improvements in sales, if not operating income, of almost 2%. The Spanish market is therefore not in top shape yet.

Brazil saw significant upside - ROI up 23.4% to almost as much as France, with continued traction from the store renovations and transformations, with the current optimization close to complete.

It's these sorts of metrics we want to keep an eye on here, aside from the key financial indicators, which have been improving as well. Not only did the company improve its dividend to €0.92/share, but it also added a special dividend of €0.23, payable at the same time. That means that, paradoxically enough, at this pricing and at this dividend, Carrefour has become a bona fide high yielder, at close to 9% yield on my cost basis.

The company's net debt increased during the year, worsening its balance sheet - but this has to do with productive acquisitions, and the net free cash flow is up to almost €1.5B, which is entirely in line with the company's plans and forecasts for its 2025-2027 period.

And the fact that the company's net sales are now at €85B per year should show you the scope and size of Carrefour, with an EBITDA that's closing in on €5B. Therefore, the company's special dividend, which amounts to €150M in total for the year, does not worry me and should not worry you either.

The company may operate in markets that, as investors in the USA and Europe, we can view as dangerous or volatile, but take it from me that these markets need solid grocery services too, and I too often see investors and analysts underestimating the income from exactly these markets. Especially telling can be the company's evolution on the French markets, which has gone the following way.

This is despite very tough environments in the remainder of Europe, leading to lower trends and operating income. The company has made significant investments in all of these markets, but these investments will take time to see results and positives. Some of the eastern European markets, especially have seen negatives from inflation, labor costs, and other trends - Romania and Poland above all - but this was weighed up by Latin American trends during this specific year.

The company's adjusted EPS more than covers the dividend, even with the extraordinary dividend. The company's main risks that I see for investing here remain things like FX. The impact on FX when we have this much emerging market currency is not to be underestimated. See the following.

Despite improvements in the home market, the company's pricing position is also still less than Leclerc, with Auchan also remaining a competitor in the nation. The company's exposure to the hypermarket format has been lessened, but it's still there, and while improving, its store brands are still less than its competitors, which is where most of its competitors are making money.

Also, if you're wondering why Carrefour is not reporting Middle East/African segments, it's because those stores and the entire market is operated by Majid Al Futtaim, which brought Carrefour in 1995, and owns the rights to wholly operate the brand in 30 countries, including Africa, Asia and the Middle East. Majid Al Futtaim operates almost 470 Carrefour stores in 13 countries, visited by almost 300M people per year. It has a franchise structure to do so (Source).

Majid Al Futtaim is based in the UAE.

Because of how the company is structured and the exposure it has, this is exactly the type of company I want to own. I will not sell or rotate until the company goes into significant overvaluation. What's more, because of its geography, Carrefour does not compete either with Walmart (WMT) or other format-similars like Tesco.

I view Carrefour as the most attractively priced, higher-yielding grocer out there today - and I will show you why that is.

Carrefour - Valuation implies a massive upside, including a very hefty yield.

1Q25 is incoming, and regardless of that quarter's results, I consider the company attractively positioned even after a 7% rise. It's my view that one should invest prior to the release of that result, which is why I am posting the article now.

Carrefour is valued at less than 8.5x P/E. This is a BBB rated BBB-rated Consumer Staples retail business with a conservative leverage yielding almost 8.7% even today. That is not something you find easily, nor is it something you find every day.

The company's typical historical multiple is 10-11x. This is still low, but I have some understanding of it, given the geographical exposure that the company has, as well as the historical trends of the company, which are far from perfect.

The upside for Carrefour is non-trivial. Even if we were to only estimate Carrefour at the current 8- 9x P/E, the upside to that is 20.8% per year, or 66% RoR until 2027E, based on the current growth estimate with a fair value PT of €19/share.

And remember, this is essentially the "bearish" case that I see here. Using something like a 20-year average, Carrefour trades at a P/E of 14-15x. If we apply this, we get a fair value of something closer to €30/share, which would imply an annualized RoR of 40% or 150% in 3 years. That's the bullish assumption, and to me, it's equally unlikely. I therefore say that the likely turnout Is somewhere in the middle, which is why I forecast the company at a 5-10 year average of 11x P/E, and you should note that this is still well below where most consumer staples companies trade.

This comes to a realistic upside of almost 30% RoR annualized, and a triple-digit 101% total RoR until 2027E, inclusive of dividends. Carrefour, in terms of its peers, is cheaply valued than both Walmart and Tesco, and has a far better yield than either of them. I use a 11x P/E forward to come to my PT of €20.5/share, and to be clear here, I am saying that it's better to invest below €20.5/share, not at this PT.

The company does have volatility both in earnings forecasts and in fundamentals, but the attractive dividend and the low price that's being asked here cushion this very well for me.

I say that as of April 2025, the company is a very attractive buy, and it should especially be bought before the first reports, as the company is still trading below €13.8/share as I am writing this article, which is where I consider Carrefour to be a cheap company, meaning it fulfills all of my criteria.

ADR is CRRFY, a 0.2x ADR, meaning that 5 CRRFY is 1 native share of Carrefour. That means my PT for CRRFY is $4.67/share as of this time.

Thesis

  • Carrefour is one of the strongest and largest hypermarket/supermarket chains in Europe. The company has gone from performing poorly since the financial crisis to performing quite well in the full year of 2024, covered here, and is expected to continue to do so moving forward into 2025-2026 and beyond, with specific growth in 2026-2027.
  • At a cheap valuation, which we have at this time, in my view, without any doubt, Carrefour deserves a place on a person's "Buy" list. It has a very high yield and a high degree of safety.
  • I expect an upside of potentially into the triple digits, making this a 60%-150%-plus RoR even after the company is above €12/share at an unchanged PT from here. My PT is €20.5/share, and this one is a "Buy" here.
  • For the ADR CRRFY, a 0.2x ADR, the PT comes to $4.67. For the ADR CRERF, a SHARE ADR (meaning 1:1), the PT is closer to $22/share. I would suggest that if you go for the ADR's, you pick the more liquid one.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative, and well run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills three out of three quality indicators and two out of two valuation indicators. Based on this, and a valuation target of €20.5/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Buy" rating as of April 2025. This is one of the rare companies that fulfills every single criterion I have.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ClearBridge Small Cap Strategy Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
By Albert Grosman & Brian Lund, CFA

Market Overview

Despite an optimistic start to the year, the threat of tariffs and trade policy changes under the new Trump administration and a rotation out of AI-related tech stocks spurred volatility and uncertainty that sent equities lower for the first quarter. A risk-off mentality weighed particularly hard on small cap stocks, generally thought to be more fragile to their larger cap peers, resulting in the benchmark Russell 2000 Index declining 9.48%, nearly 500 basis points worse than the large cap Russell 1000 Index and over 500 basis points behind than the S&P 500 Index. Concerns over the economic ramifications of new tariffs, declining consumer sentiment and obfuscation of the trajectory of interest rates and earnings helped value stocks seize leadership from growth stocks with the Russell 2000 Value Index returning -7.74% compared to the -11.12% of the Russell 2000 Growth Index.

Portfolio Performance

Amid this turmoil, the ClearBridge Small Cap Strategy outperformed its benchmark in the first quarter as strong selection in the health care and consumer discretionary sectors helped overcome detractors among our industrial holdings.

Despite the prospect of higher rates and continued policy uncertainty from new and controversial leadership at the Department of Health and Human Services, the health care sector was the primary contributor to both absolute and relative returns and contained three of our top five individual contributors: Corcept Therapeutics (CORT), Intra-Cellular Therapies (ITCI) and Verona Pharma (VRNA). Corcept, which manufacturers drugs used to treat endocrinologic, neurologic disorders as well as cancer, saw its share price surge late in the quarter after it announced positive data from a Phase 3 trial for an ovarian cancer indication of its drug relacorilant. This represents a potentially significant market expansion opportunity, and further positive data may propel its drug to become a leading treatment for ovarian cancer. Intra-Cellular Therapies, a biopharmaceutical company that develops treatments for central nervous system disorders, also saw an increase after it announced its intention to be acquired by Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) for a more than 50% premium to its share price; we exited our shares following the announcement. Finally, Verona Pharma rose on strong earnings that showed an increase in fourth quarter and 2024 sales figures for its lung disease therapy, Ohtuvayre, which was launched in August, and which we believe can capture a significant proportion of the $10 billion Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) market.

Stock selection within consumer discretionary also positively contributed to performance, led by online educational curriculum, software and solutions company Stride. In addition to posting positive fourth-quarter earnings, with both revenue and earnings above analyst expectations, the company continues to see solid enrollment growth, especially in its career learning offerings. We believe the company’s solid execution on its post-COVID online education growth objectives has demonstrated its ability to win and maintain new enrollments and that the continued expansion of its career learning offerings — tailored to the unique career interest of its members — remains an attractive opportunity for future growth.

Stock selection in the industrials sector was the primary detractor during the quarter, mainly due to Allegiant Travel (ALGT) — an airline and hospitality company specifically catering to leisure travelers. Despite its airline revenue exceeding market expectations for the fourth quarter, the company’s share price declined after announcing it was reducing the value of its Sunseeker Resort. Resignation of its Chief Operating Officer in early March and economic uncertainty may put additional pressure on consumer spending — particularly with regard to leisure and discretionary purchases. Primoris Services (PRIM), which provides a range of construction, maintenance and infrastructure engineering services, also saw its share price decline after the announcement that President Trump would cease spending on the proposed $300 billion in green infrastructure legislation enacted under the Biden administration, hampering the growth projections of the company’s energy division.

Portfolio Positioning

We swapped our exposure in WaFd for Columbia Banking System (COLB), the holding company for Umpqua Bank, which provides banking, mortgage and other financial services. We believe Columbia has a number of advantages over WaFd: a more attractive valuation, more consolidated geographic footprint, more diversified and higher yielding loan book; better established lending base is and consistently higher net interest margin. Additionally, we believe that Columbia will generate meaningful synergies and tangible book value recoveries from loans written down on its 2023 merger with Umpqua.

We also adjusted our positioning within health care by adding Privia (PRVA) and exiting Lantheus (LNTH). Privia offers a unique health care platform (including technology infrastructure, centralized shared services, access to broader care) that reduces physicians’ administrative work and improves clinical integration, resulting in better health care services and outcomes for patients. The company has successfully been able to scale its platform, avoiding the pitfalls of competitors, and thus has become a preferred partner for independent health care providers. Meanwhile, Lantheus, which develops diagnostic and therapeutic products that assist clinicians in the diagnosis and treatment of heart, cancer and other diseases, faces increased concerns over changes in the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services reimbursement rates. Additionally, we believe that increased competition to the company’s Pylarify diagnostic imaging agent could negatively impact margins.

Outlook

The outlook for small caps is muddied by uncertainty about the U.S. economy under new tariff rules, just as it is for large caps. The difference is that small caps have ended the first quarter in the bottom decile of relative valuation versus large caps after years of underperformance. Since tariffs were announced, that difference has only widened. While small cap businesses are thought to be generally more fragile than their larger-cap counterparts, they are also more domestically focused, meaning that in a prolonged trade war, they may turn out to be even more attractive at these valuations.

Portfolio Highlights

The ClearBridge Small Cap Strategy outperformed its Russell 2000 Index benchmark during the first quarter. On an absolute basis, the Strategy had losses in nine of the 11 sectors in which it was invested during the quarter. The leading contributor was the health care sector, while the industrials and information technology sectors were the main detractors.

On a relative basis, overall stock selection and sector allocation effects contributed to performance. Stock selection in the health care, consumer discretionary, materials and IT sectors, as well as an underweight to the IT sector benefited performance. Conversely, stock selection in the industrials and financials sectors weighed on performance.

On an individual stock basis, the biggest contributors to absolute returns in the quarter were Corcept Therapeutics, Intra-Cellular Therapies, MP Materials (MP), Verona Pharma and Stride (LRN). The largest detractors were Allegiant Travel, PROG (PRG), Encore Capital (ECPG), Scholar Rock (SRRK) and Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (ARWR).

In addition to the transactions listed above, we initiated new positions in ASGN (ASGN), Zeta Global (ZETA) and TeraWulf (WULF) in the IT sector, Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals and Beta Bionics (BBNX) in the health care sector and Green Plains (GPRE) in the energy sector. We exited positions in HF Sinclair (DINO) in the energy sector, nLIGHT (LASR) and indie Semiconductor (INDI) in the IT sector and EnerSys (ENS) in the industrials sector.

Albert Grosman, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager

Brian Lund, CFA, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager
