data.title: Old Dominion: Light At The End Of The Tunnel
data.description:
It has been dark days indeed for US truckers. After the pandemic-driven boom caused freight rates to skyrocket and attracted new capacity (a problem in this low-barrier-to-entry industry); everything has gone downhill. At several points in the last couple of years, trucking managers have devised a light at the end of the tunnel…only to see that tunnel grow longer.

The situation has not spared the LTL segment. Although not a big portion of the Cass Freight Index, we know from the recent update at industry leader Old Dominion Freight Lines (ODFL) (as well as competitor JB Hunt, which reported weak numbers) that dawn has yet to arrive for LTL carriers. In their Q1 interim update, OD CEO noted a continued slow economy is still driving revenues lower.

The Cass Freight index is now showing almost 30 months of consecutive volume drops. The two charts below demonstrate the lengthy duration of the current downdraft:

In volume terms, there is no precedent for such a long-drawn-out slowdown. Through this process, there have been exits – Yellow’s liquidation the most prominent – but still capacity is not yet in balance with demand and hence pricing softness remains.

As for pricing, although the last few months show slight relative improvement – and perhaps promoting those hopes that the end is nearing – what we really need to see to breathe easier is a recovery in volumes.

Volume levels haven't grown in decades, reflecting a shift to services in the economy. Freight rate levels are nothing to cheer about either. We are right at 3x the base-year (1990). A tripling in 35 years implies rates have increased at just above 3% rate. Hardly beating inflation and once again highlighting the difficulties in earning superior returns in such a competitive industry.

Old Dominion stands out anyway

Despite the challenging backdrop, superior returns is just what Old Dominion does best. They have done it through superior capital allocation, negligible use of debt and above all superior customer service. As shared in my prior writeup, OD has sustained superior returns on capital invested, as well as expanding market share, both are sure characteristics of superior competitive position (a moat).

Since that previous article, the stock is (16%) lower, and (14%) YTD. My long-term view on the company’s prospects remains steadfast, however, and lower prices should be seen more as an opportunity than a cause for grief.

Not only can we buy more shares at lower multiples, but the company itself is doing the same, share repurchases have been a major contributor to growth in earnings per share and the main focus of their capital return policy.

In the meantime, ODFL continues to fight for market share, with the industry’s best performance, intentionally maintaining additional network spare capacity in anticipation of the eventual recovery in volumes (when a meaningful slice of market share comes up for grabs). Cyclical industries favor the prepared.

Outlook for the industry

As they say, the trick to forecasting is to call out what is going to happen but not say when. Keeping true to such advice, we should take comfort in the fact that a recovery is indeed going to happen – we just don’t know when.

The difficulty in spotting the turning point can be a problem for investors tracking an index quarter by quarter, but it should not concern long-term capital allocators. A growing economy almost by definition implies growing activity within it. For a long time, most of the growth has been away from manufacturing, however that trend may have run its course.

Pandemic closures highlighted the perils of outsourcing critical production. China is the world's factory and will continue to be for the foreseeable future, there is no point in bringing back toy-making. But some manufacturing is coming back onshore - inefficiencies be damned.

The LTL shipping industry likes to see manufacturing growth. Especially small and mid-sized manufacturing companies that lack scale and distribution capacity, and therefore rely on LTL for meeting client orders and bringing in supplies.

Regardless of one's view's on the current administration's priorities, it is perfectly clear that their policies are intended to “bring back” Made in America and rebuild some of the hollowed-out manufacturing base.

It is unclear how much success these efforts will meet, but certainly some things will change. Local manufacturers that used to compete with imported goods, will benefit. Despite their obvious downsides, fostering local production is always the justification for tariffs (and they are effective at that).

Some manufacturing re-shoring may follow. Even if tariff negotiations end up lowering the overall rates intended to come in by summer, all supply chain managers now realize that globalization (and offshoring in particular) have limits and relying on them poses a greater risk going forward.

On the one hand, Foxconn-sized operations cannot be “re-shored” overnight (or over many nights). On the other hand, not all customers are as big as Apple. Smaller brands will think harder about manufacturing abroad, increasing demand for local installed capacity.

Current valuation

The current market turmoil has affected LTL peers harder than OD. Perhaps in recognition of their superior margins and no net-debt. All trade at similar multiples, I expect even if we are heading into a recession (a full economy recession, beyond the freight recession), OD will do better than peers and continue leading in profitability metrics.

And the valuation is getting more attractive. This is about as low a PE multiple as we have seen in the last three years.

In relation to the market, the multiples are high. Deservedly so. The company has achieved average 18% returns on equity for more than 30 years. They have done it by providing unmatched service and market share gains. It is possible to differentiate even in apparently commoditized industries (OD is proof).

Today, the company stands ready with spare capacity in their service centers to accommodate the return of growth. This is how they gain new customers that come to them when tighter competitors reach capacity (and some never go back). There are costs of running with spare capacity, but the rewards are worth it.

During the Q4 call, CFO Adam Satterfield answered a question about service center openings the following way:

We're at 261 service centers today and we continue to execute on our long term CapEx plan really over the balance of the last couple of years, like Marty mentioned. And so we had four openings overall last year. And as we look out this year, we're a little north of 30% from an excess capacity standpoint. So unless demand really dictates, we don't have to open any in that regard."

Thirty percent is a very wide margin of safety. The costs are borne today, lowering operating rations from where they would otherwise be, but that also means there is significant operating leverage there when demand picks up.

Wrap up

Cass Freight Indexes continue to paint a bleak picture for the industry. The slump in volumes that started late in 2022 is still with us. Every monthly print in the red is now a new record; as prior downturns had lasted less than two years.

Through it all, industry leader OD has managed to stay ahead in profitability and is able to continue to generate excess cash flow and repurchase shares. Those buybacks should accelerate, as real estate capex (usually around half of all capex) will be limited by already significant spare capacity.

We can never know for sure what will spark a recovery. It could be retailer inventory replenishing, manufacturing demand returning or a combination of both. Recent policy shifts might help, but other recoveries have occurred without them too, and we are overdue for one. No tunnel lasts forever.

data.title: MAGS: The End Of An Era (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The recent market crash has prompted a reassessment of the old order of things. One subject under scrutiny is the "Mag 7" group of stocks represented by the Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF (MAGS). These were the market leaders for many years, but turned into laggards this year. Furthermore, they no longer look like a coherent group of stocks. Tesla (TSLA) has always been the odd one out, both by sector and performance, but the trade war with China is likely to weigh on some stocks more than others. This article looks at why MAGS may no longer be relevant and why investors could find alpha in stock selection.

MAGS - The Basic Facts

MAGS is the only ETF to provide equal-weight exposure to the Mag 7 group of stocks, although that's not immediately clear when you see the fund has 26 positions and US Treasuries as its top holding.

The added complexity is due to diversification rules, as the prospectus explains,

The Fund utilizes total return swaps in order to maintain compliance with RIC diversification tests. The Fund intends to qualify as a Regulated Investment Company for tax purposes.

It is also addressed in a Roundhill blog, which expands on the specific rule they are trying to bypass.

Positions exceeding 5% cannot in aggregate exceed 50% of the fund’s total assets.

Using swaps frees up some of the AUM to invest in UST Bills, which provide a special dividend at the end of the year. Last December the distribution was $0.44, which is less than 1%, so nothing especially attractive.

MAGS is an actively managed fund, which seems odd as it only really rebalances every quarter to maintain equal weighting. I assume the various swaps and UST positions also need management. In any case, the expense ratio of 0.29% seems a bit steep, but MAGS is the only ETF of this type and the AUM has grown to $1.76B.

Performance - From Leaders to Laggards

MAGS was launched in 2023, so there is limited data to test performance. What little we do have, shows it has significantly outperformed benchmarks such as the Nasdaq 100 (QQQ). The 2022–2025 rally marked the height of the "Mag 7" dominance, where the broader market (SPY) was largely propelled by the outsized gains of these standout stocks.

There were warnings that this dominance was not healthy and better breadth was needed for a sustained bull market. 2025 YTD has proven this theory correct as the above chart has been flipped on its head.

There is very little data available on risk-adjusted returns as MAGS only traded for one full year, 2024. Unsurprisingly, the numbers are amazing and MAGS has a Sortino ratio of 14.63.

2025 is going to be very different, and the maximum drawdown is already over 20%. With only 7 stocks and equal weighting, there are stock-specific risks. Tesla is -40% YTD, so even if four of the other stocks gained 10%, the net gain would be zero. This is one of the reasons I think stock selection is preferable to holding this ETF.

Slowing Growth

There are various reasons for the sluggish performance in 2025, which started before the trade war. The first chart below shows the Mag 7 recovered from an earnings recession in 2023 to grow EPS as much as 60% while the other 493 companies in the S&P 500 had negative earnings growth. The gap between the two groups of stocks is narrowing this year and is expected to be as little as 2% by Q4 as Mag 7 earnings growth slows.

The second chart shows that the valuations at the beginning of the year were unusually high and in no way attractive, especially in light of the slowing earnings growth.

A New Era

The drop of over 20% in 2025 has helped lower valuations, and earnings growth is expected to be a healthy 20% for the coming quarters. MAGS is more attractive than it was, and holds some of the most successful and profitable companies in the world. But that doesn't mean it will revert to its old ways and lead a new rally.

Firstly, the outperformance in the 2023-2024 period was driven partly by the AI megatrend, which centered around Nvidia (NVDA). It grew its market cap by over 500% as demand for semiconductors surged.

NVDA will continue to be a great company, but that kind of price growth is very unlikely to repeat now that it is valued at $2.5T. Furthermore, the ban on H20 exports to China has led to approximately $5.5 billion of charges associated with H20 products for inventory, purchase commitments, and related reserves.

Other Mag 7 companies have similar headwinds from the trade war. Apple (AAPL) was faced with 145% tariffs on imports from China, and while these have been reduced to 20% on specific goods such as smartphones and computers, that is still a big hike and the administration has warned there will be new semiconductor tariffs implemented in a "month or two." AAPL sells 200 million iPhones a year and 90% are imported from China. Additional 20% costs on imports will hurt margins, and Apple can either pass some of the costs on to consumers or absorb them. Either way, the situation is troubling.

Amazon (AMZN) will also be severely affected by tariffs. Domestic sellers who rely on Chinese imports will struggle to absorb the 145% tariff and passing it on to consumers through price hikes will hurt demand. Meanwhile, Chinese sellers will face similar problems, and they make up a large part of the Amazon trade.

China is home to around half of Amazon's sellers, with over 100,000 Amazon businesses registered in the southern city of Shenzhen alone, generating annual revenues of $35.3 billion, according to e-commerce services provider SmartScout.

Altogether, around 71% of the items sold on Amazon are produced in China.

Company guidance will be very interesting when they report earnings on May 1st. The 145% tariffs were only announced last week, so analysts are still busy calculating their impact, but given the percentage of goods imported and the size of the tariffs, Amazon could face a big drop-off in demand and revenue. AMZN is -21% YTD despite the bounce last week.

NVDA, AAPL and AMZN all have a China problem. Tesla also has Chinese exposure, but its main problem is falling demand for its EV. Deliveries dropped 13% YoY in its last release.

Tesla reported it produced over 362,000 vehicles and delivered over 336,000 in the first quarter of 2025. That performance marked a decline compared to the same period one year ago, when Tesla produced over 433,000 vehicles and delivered about 387,000.

TSLA has always been the odd one out in the Mag 7 as it stayed at lower highs from 2021 to late 2024 and did not participate in the 2023-2024 MAGS rally. Its -40% YTD performance again sets it apart.

The two stocks in the communication sector, Meta (META) and Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) are less at threat from tariffs, although they are still at risk from an economic slowdown. Microsoft (MSFT) also looks fairly well insulated. These stocks may show stability when compared to the other four stocks in MAGS and grouping all seven disparate stocks together no longer seems logical. I don't think the term "Mag 7" will survive 2025 and will fall out of common use, a bit FAANG did. This is a new era, and new leaders will eventually emerge.

Conclusions

MAGS launched in 2023 and captured the dominant period of the Mag 7. That period is over, and I don't think it will be repeated. 2025 has brought massive changes to markets and the underlying drivers, and MAGS has flipped from a leader to a laggard. I rate MAGS a "sell," as owning all the Mag 7 in equal weights is unlikely to deliver alpha when the stocks are diverging due to different drivers.

data.title: Treasury Yields Running Amok Amidst Tariff Madness
data.description:
The Trump administration wanted to get bond yields down, but yields have skyrocketed more than in over four decades. Investors are jittery about holding US debt in response to Trump’s trade wars and, possibly, the idea of a president who is pushing a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve in what would amount to a bid against the US dollar.

Volatility signals a rattled market. The 10-year yield surged more than it has since 1982, signalling that holders of long-term US debt are dumping their positions. The higher yields go, the more you’re paid to hold the debt because markets perceive a higher level of risk. Once seen as the ultimate stable milquetoast investment, the yield surge from a crash to 3.9% to over 4.5% in recent days has caused investors to question holding long-term US debt.

10-Year Treasury Yields, 1-Month

In fact, Trumpenomic chaos has cast a shadow of doubt on the US dollar itself. That sentiment is being reflected in the US dollar index, which was boosted significantly after Trump was elected. During the period before Trump took office, currency speculators who thought that a Trump win would boost the dollar placed their bets and pumped the price. Their bets have soured as Trump began introducing and implementing his economic agenda.

DXY 1-Month

The administration’s inability to keep yields in check is raising serious questions about the future of U.S. debt and the broader economy. The catalyst has been Trump’s aggressive tariff strategy, which aims to protect American businesses and boost the long-dead domestic manufacturing sector. Foreign holders, including major players like China and Japan, are reconsidering their positions, fearing that the U.S. is becoming a riskier bet in an increasingly unpredictable trade environment. The Trump administration knows that there is an event horizon for Treasury yields, at which a cascade of debt-selling starts that can’t be stopped. That’s Trump and Co. backtracked on their tariff plans.

But if the Fed doesn’t start QE, the Treasury will do it for them in a desperate bid to keep pushing Treasury yields down. It will buy back its own debt in a mathematically confounding fiscal ouroboros. Though Keynesians will insist that Treasury buybacks aren’t QE, despite being a way to increase liquidity. But buybacks can function as QE by another name, fundamentally still expanding the money supply and fueling inflation through fiscal operations rather than monetary policy.

If this trend continues, we could be staring down the barrel of a financial crisis that makes the 2008 meltdown look tame.

The Fed may have no choice but to step in with emergency purchases of Treasuries to stabilize the market. QE “worked” during the pandemic and the 2008 crisis by injecting liquidity and keeping borrowing costs low by kicking the can down the road and fueling inflation that would lead to today’s sky-high consumer prices. What’s more, it could further inflate the already staggering U.S. debt, trillions of which will soon need to be serviced. The pressure is on.

The U.S. could face a vicious cycle of rising debt servicing costs, higher inflation, and slower growth — a stagflation scenario that could derail Trump’s economic agenda and alienate his core voter base. The resulting financial crisis could dwarf 2008.

The upside-down bond market isn’t just a thermometer for economic health; it’s a disciplinarian. But the true “safe haven” was never the 10-year bond, which assumes that at any given time, a decade from now, the US will be in good standing to pay its debts. The true safe haven is gold.

Higher bond yields historically correspond with a higher dollar. You have to buy dollars to invest in Treasurys, but the Trump administration’s tariff madness has broken the correlation as investors take a dim view on the value of the currency itself as well as the debt that it’s linked to. The fragility of the US economy is on full display as a full-blown collapse looms.

We may now be at a crossroads where economic chickens, from past and present, all at once flock home to roost

Original Post
